questions.question: Can an object have zero displacement even after travelling a non-zero distance? Explain with an example.
questions.answer: Yes. When an object returns to its starting point, displacement = 0 but distance ≠ 0.
questions.solution:
Displacement = shortest straight-line distance from initial to final position. If the final position coincides with the initial position, displacement = 0.
Example: A boy walks 60 m from O to A, then walks back 60 m from A to O. Distance covered = 60 + 60 = 120 m. Displacement = 0 m (started and ended at O).
Another example: an athlete completes one full lap of a circular track — distance = 2πr, displacement = 0.

questions.question: A car travels along a rectangular path: 40 m east, 30 m north, 40 m west, 30 m south, returning to start. What is the distance covered and the displacement?
questions.answer: Distance = 40 + 30 + 40 + 30 = 140 m. Displacement = 0 m (returns to starting point).
questions.solution:
Distance: sum of all path lengths = 40 + 30 + 40 + 30 = 140 m.
Displacement: the car starts and ends at the same point → final position = initial position → displacement = 0 m.
This shows: distance is always the actual path length (140 m), displacement is the straight-line gap between start and end (0 m). Both distance and displacement have the same unit (m).

questions.question: What does the odometer of a vehicle measure — distance or displacement? Justify.
questions.answer: Odometer measures distance (total path length), not displacement.
questions.solution:
An odometer records every metre of path the vehicle travels, regardless of direction. It counts total path length, including all curves and back-and-forth movement.
Displacement would be the straight-line distance from the journey's start to end — the odometer has no way to record this (it doesn't track direction or final position relative to start).
Example: A car drives 1850 km from Bhubaneshwar to New Delhi via a curved road. Odometer reads 1850 km (distance). The actual straight-line displacement between the two cities is less than 1850 km.

questions.question: Under what condition is the magnitude of displacement equal to the distance travelled by an object?
questions.answer: When the object moves along a straight line in one direction only, without reversing.
questions.solution:
Displacement (magnitude) = shortest straight-line distance from initial to final position.
Distance = total path length.
These are equal ONLY when the entire path is a straight line and the object never reverses direction — every metre of path directly contributes to the straight-line gap from start to end.
Examples:
• Object moving from O to A on a straight road without turning back → distance = displacement magnitude.
• Car driving straight 60 km north → distance = 60 km = displacement magnitude.
But: car drives 40 km east, then 30 km north → distance = 70 km; displacement magnitude = √(40²+30²) = 50 km → not equal.

questions.question: Distinguish between speed and velocity with one example each.
questions.answer: Speed: scalar — only magnitude (distance/time). Velocity: vector — magnitude + direction (displacement/time).
questions.solution:
Speed = distance covered per unit time. Scalar — only magnitude.
Formula: speed = total distance / total time. SI unit: m s⁻¹.
Velocity = displacement per unit time. Vector — magnitude AND direction.
Formula: velocity = total displacement / total time. SI unit: m s⁻¹.

Examples:
• Speed: 'The car is moving at 60 km h⁻¹' — only how fast.
• Velocity: 'The car is moving at 60 km h⁻¹ due north' — how fast and which direction.

Key consequence: average speed ≥ |average velocity| always. For Usha swimming 180 m (90 m + 90 m back) in 60 s: average speed = 3 m s⁻¹, average velocity = 0/60 = 0 m s⁻¹ (displacement = 0).

questions.question: An object travels 16 m in 4 s and then another 16 m in 2 s. Calculate its average speed.
questions.answer: Average speed = 32/6 = 5.33 m s⁻¹.
questions.solution:
Total distance = 16 m + 16 m = 32 m.
Total time = 4 s + 2 s = 6 s.
Average speed = total distance / total time = 32/6 = 5.33 m s⁻¹.
Note: we cannot say the instantaneous speed was constant throughout — the object was faster in the second leg (16 m in 2 s = 8 m s⁻¹) than the first (16 m in 4 s = 4 m s⁻¹). Average speed gives the effective rate over the whole journey.

questions.question: When will you say a body is in (i) uniform acceleration? (ii) non-uniform acceleration?
questions.answer: (i) Uniform acceleration: velocity changes by equal amounts in equal intervals of time. (ii) Non-uniform acceleration: velocity changes by unequal amounts in equal intervals of time.
questions.solution:
(i) Uniform (constant) acceleration: the velocity of the object increases or decreases by the same amount every second. Example: a freely falling body — gains 10 m s⁻¹ every second under gravity (on Earth, ignoring air resistance).
(ii) Non-uniform acceleration: the rate of change of velocity itself changes — it is not constant. Example: a car in city traffic — sometimes speeding up fast, sometimes slowly, sometimes decelerating.

Key test: If the velocity-time graph is a STRAIGHT LINE → uniform acceleration. If it is a CURVE → non-uniform acceleration.

questions.question: What is the nature of the distance-time graph for (i) uniform motion, (ii) non-uniform motion, and (iii) an object at rest?
questions.answer: (i) Straight inclined line (positive slope). (ii) Curved line. (iii) Horizontal straight line (parallel to time axis).
questions.solution:
(i) Uniform motion: equal distances in equal time → distance is directly proportional to time → graph is a straight line with a positive slope. Slope of this line = constant speed.
(ii) Non-uniform motion: unequal distances in equal time → graph is a curve (non-linear). If the object is accelerating, the curve bends upward (increasing slope = increasing speed).
(iii) Object at rest: distance does not change with time → graph is a horizontal straight line (slope = 0 = speed).

Key rule: Slope of distance-time graph = speed at that instant.

questions.question: What does the area under a velocity-time graph represent?
questions.answer: The area under the velocity-time graph represents the distance (magnitude of displacement) covered by the object in the given time interval.
questions.solution:
From the definition: distance = velocity × time.
On a v-t graph: the 'area' of the region between the graph line and the x-axis = height (velocity) × base (time) = displacement/distance.

Specific cases:
• Uniform velocity (horizontal line): area = rectangle = v × t = distance.
• Uniform acceleration (straight inclined line): area = trapezoid = area of rectangle (u×t) + area of triangle (½×(v−u)×t) = ut + ½at².
• This is exactly the second equation of motion: s = ut + ½at².

Full area under any v-t graph (using integration concept) = total distance covered.

questions.question: State the three equations of motion. What are the symbols used, and when can these equations be applied?
questions.answer: v = u + at; s = ut + ½at²; v² = u² + 2as. Applied only for uniform (constant) acceleration along a straight line.
questions.solution:
Symbols: u = initial velocity (m s⁻¹), v = final velocity (m s⁻¹), a = acceleration (m s⁻²), t = time (s), s = distance covered (m).

1. v = u + at: gives final velocity after time t. Derived from definition of acceleration.
2. s = ut + ½at²: gives distance covered in time t.
3. v² = u² + 2as: gives final velocity after covering distance s. Eliminates t.

Conditions: ONLY valid when:
(a) Acceleration is constant (uniform) throughout the motion.
(b) Motion is along a straight line.
NOT valid for circular motion, varying acceleration, or 2D motion (directly).

questions.question: Why is uniform circular motion considered accelerated motion even though the speed is constant?
questions.answer: Because velocity is a vector. Even at constant speed, the direction of motion changes at every point on the circle — this change in velocity direction means the object is accelerating.
questions.solution:
Velocity = speed + direction (vector quantity).
In uniform circular motion:
• Speed (magnitude of velocity): CONSTANT.
• Direction of velocity: changes continuously at every point (always tangential to the circle).
Since velocity (the vector) is changing (direction changes), there IS a rate of change of velocity → there IS acceleration.
This acceleration is called centripetal acceleration and is always directed toward the centre of the circle.

Proof from Activity 7.11 (stone on thread): when released, the stone moves in a straight line along the tangent — at the moment of release, this was the direction of its velocity. This means the velocity direction was always changing while it moved in a circle.

questions.question: A car's odometer reads 2000 km at the start and 2400 km at the end of a trip lasting 8 hours. Find average speed in km h⁻¹ and m s⁻¹.
questions.answer: Average speed = 50 km h⁻¹ = 13.9 m s⁻¹.
questions.solution:
Distance covered = 2400 − 2000 = 400 km.
Time = 8 h.
Average speed = distance / time = 400/8 = 50 km h⁻¹.

Conversion to m s⁻¹:
50 km h⁻¹ × (1000 m / 1 km) × (1 h / 3600 s) = 50 × 1000/3600 = 50 × 5/18 = 13.9 m s⁻¹.

Note: This is the average speed over the whole trip. The car might have gone faster or slower at various points — the average smooths these variations.

questions.question: A bus starts from rest and moves with uniform acceleration of 0.1 m s⁻² for 2 minutes. Find (a) the speed acquired and (b) the distance travelled.
questions.solution:
Given: u = 0 (starts from rest), a = 0.1 m s⁻², t = 2 min = 120 s.
(a) Using v = u + at:
v = 0 + 0.1 × 120 = 12 m s⁻¹.

(b) Using s = ut + ½at²:
s = 0 × 120 + ½ × 0.1 × 120²
s = 0 + 0.05 × 14400
s = 720 m.

Verification using v² = u² + 2as: 12² = 0 + 2 × 0.1 × s → s = 144/0.2 = 720 m. ✓

questions.question: A train is travelling at 90 km h⁻¹. Brakes produce a uniform acceleration of −0.5 m s⁻². How far will the train go before stopping?
questions.solution:
Given: u = 90 km h⁻¹ = 90 × (5/18) = 25 m s⁻¹; a = −0.5 m s⁻²; v = 0 (stops).

Using v² = u² + 2as:
0 = 25² + 2 × (−0.5) × s
0 = 625 − s
s = 625 m.

Time to stop: from v = u + at → 0 = 25 + (−0.5)t → t = 50 s.

Physical insight: a heavily loaded train at high speed requires a very long braking distance (625 m ≈ over half a kilometre). This is why trains need to apply brakes well in advance of stations.

questions.question: A car accelerates uniformly from 18 km h⁻¹ to 36 km h⁻¹ in 5 s. Calculate (i) the acceleration and (ii) the distance covered.
questions.solution:
Convert velocities: u = 18 × 5/18 = 5 m s⁻¹; v = 36 × 5/18 = 10 m s⁻¹; t = 5 s.

(i) a = (v − u)/t = (10 − 5)/5 = 5/5 = 1 m s⁻².

(ii) Using s = ut + ½at²:
s = 5 × 5 + ½ × 1 × 5²
s = 25 + 12.5
s = 37.5 m.

Alternative using v² = u² + 2as: 100 = 25 + 2 × 1 × s → s = 75/2 = 37.5 m. ✓

questions.question: Brakes applied to a car produce an acceleration of 6 m s⁻² in the opposite direction to motion. If the car takes 2 s to stop, calculate (i) the initial speed and (ii) the stopping distance.
questions.answer: (i) u = 12 m s⁻¹. (ii) s = 12 m.
questions.solution:
Given: a = −6 m s⁻² (deceleration), t = 2 s, v = 0 (stops).

(i) Using v = u + at:
0 = u + (−6) × 2 → u = 12 m s⁻¹.

(ii) Using s = ut + ½at²:
s = 12 × 2 + ½ × (−6) × 4
s = 24 − 12 = 12 m.

Physical reasoning: the car still travels 12 m forward after brakes are applied before stopping. This is the stopping distance or braking distance. This explains why drivers must maintain a safe following distance — a car cannot stop instantly.

questions.question: A train starting from rest attains a velocity of 72 km h⁻¹ in 5 minutes. Find (i) acceleration and (ii) distance travelled. Assume uniform acceleration.
A stone is thrown vertically upward with velocity 5 m s⁻¹. Acceleration = 10 m s⁻² downward. Find (i) maximum height reached and (ii) time taken to reach maximum height.
questions.answer: (i) Maximum height = 1.25 m. (ii) Time = 0.5 s.
questions.solution:
Given: u = 5 m s⁻¹ (upward), a = −10 m s⁻² (downward, opposing motion), v = 0 (at maximum height).

(i) Using v² = u² + 2as:
0 = 5² + 2 × (−10) × s
0 = 25 − 20s
s = 25/20 = 1.25 m.

(ii) Using v = u + at:
0 = 5 + (−10)t
t = 5/10 = 0.5 s.

Reasoning: At maximum height, velocity = 0 (stone momentarily stops before falling back). Gravity always acts downward (−10 m s⁻²), slowing the stone on the way up.

questions.question: A ball is dropped from a height of 20 m. Its velocity increases uniformly at 10 m s⁻² (acceleration due to gravity). With what velocity does it strike the ground, and in what time?
questions.answer: v = 20 m s⁻¹. t = 2 s.
questions.solution:
Given: u = 0 (dropped from rest), a = 10 m s⁻², s = 20 m.

Finding velocity using v² = u² + 2as:
v² = 0 + 2 × 10 × 20 = 400
v = 20 m s⁻¹.

Finding time using v = u + at:
20 = 0 + 10 × t
t = 2 s.

Verification using s = ut + ½at²: s = 0 + ½ × 10 × 4 = 20 m. ✓

Reasoning: A freely falling body (ignoring air resistance) has constant acceleration = g = 10 m s⁻² downward. Every second, it gains 10 m s⁻¹ of speed.

questions.question: An athlete completes one round of a circular track of diameter 200 m in 40 s. What is (i) the distance covered and (ii) the displacement after one complete round? What is the speed?
questions.answer: (i) Distance = 200π ≈ 628 m. (ii) Displacement = 0 m. Speed = 200π/40 = 5π ≈ 15.7 m s⁻¹.
questions.solution:
Radius r = diameter/2 = 200/2 = 100 m.

(i) Distance for one full round = circumference = 2πr = 2 × π × 100 = 200π ≈ 628.3 m.

(ii) After one complete round, the athlete returns to the starting position. Final position = initial position. Displacement = 0 m.

Speed = circumference / time = 200π / 40 = 5π ≈ 15.7 m s⁻¹.

Velocity (average): displacement/time = 0/40 = 0 m s⁻¹ for one complete round.
(Instantaneous velocity is never zero — the athlete is always moving — but average velocity over a full lap is zero.)

questions.question: A velocity-time graph shows a straight line passing through the origin to the point (5 s, 10 m s⁻¹). (i) What type of motion does this represent? (ii) Calculate acceleration. (iii) Calculate distance covered.
questions.answer: (i) Uniform acceleration from rest. (ii) a = 2 m s⁻². (iii) s = 25 m.
questions.solution:
(i) Straight line on v-t graph → uniform (constant) acceleration. Starting from origin means u = 0 (starts from rest).

(ii) Acceleration = slope of v-t graph = rise/run = (10 − 0)/(5 − 0) = 10/5 = 2 m s⁻².

(iii) Distance = area under v-t graph = area of triangle (since starts from 0):
= ½ × base × height = ½ × 5 × 10 = 25 m.

Verification using s = ut + ½at²: s = 0 + ½ × 2 × 25 = 25 m. ✓

Links graphical method to equations of motion — both give the same result.

questions.question: A trolley on an inclined plane starts from rest and has an acceleration of 2 cm s⁻². What will be its velocity after 3 s?
questions.answer: v = 6 cm s⁻¹ = 0.06 m s⁻¹.
questions.solution:
Given: u = 0 (starts from rest), a = 2 cm s⁻² = 0.02 m s⁻², t = 3 s.

Using v = u + at:
v = 0 + 2 × 3 = 6 cm s⁻¹ = 0.06 m s⁻¹.

Distance covered: s = ut + ½at² = 0 + ½ × 2 × 9 = 9 cm = 0.09 m.

Physical context: The inclined plane provides a component of gravity as the driving force. As the trolley moves down, it keeps gaining speed — typical of uniformly accelerated motion along an incline.

questions.question: A motorboat accelerates from rest at 3.0 m s⁻² for 8 s. (i) Find the distance covered. (ii) If after 8 s the engine is cut and the boat decelerates at 1.5 m s⁻², how much further does it travel before stopping?
questions.answer: (i) s₁ = 96 m. (ii) s₂ = 192 m. Total = 288 m.
questions.solution:
Phase 1 (acceleration): u = 0, a = 3.0 m s⁻², t = 8 s.
v₁ = u + at = 0 + 3 × 8 = 24 m s⁻¹.
s₁ = ut + ½at² = 0 + ½ × 3 × 64 = 96 m.

Phase 2 (deceleration to rest): u₂ = 24 m s⁻¹, a = −1.5 m s⁻², v = 0.
Using v² = u² + 2as:
0 = 24² + 2 × (−1.5) × s₂
0 = 576 − 3s₂
s₂ = 192 m.

Time to stop: v = u + at → 0 = 24 − 1.5t → t = 16 s.

Total distance = s₁ + s₂ = 96 + 192 = 288 m.
Insight: the deceleration phase takes longer and covers greater distance than the acceleration phase — because deceleration rate (1.5 m s⁻²) is only half the acceleration rate (3.0 m s⁻²).

questions.question: Joseph jogs from A to B (300 m) in 2 min 30 s, then turns and jogs 100 m back to C in 1 min. Find (a) average speed and (b) average velocity for the entire journey A to C.
questions.answer: (a) Average speed = 2 m s⁻¹. (b) Average velocity = 200/210 ≈ 0.95 m s⁻¹ (toward B).
questions.solution:
Total distance = 300 + 100 = 400 m.
Total time = 2 min 30 s + 1 min = 3 min 30 s = 210 s.

(a) Average speed = total distance / total time = 400/210 ≈ 1.90 m s⁻¹.
(Note: some textbooks get exactly 2 m s⁻¹ — 400/200. Check time: 2 min 30 s = 150 s + 60 s = 210 s total → 400/210 = 1.90 m s⁻¹.)

(b) Displacement (A to C): C is 300 − 100 = 200 m from A (toward B direction).
Average velocity = displacement / time = 200/210 ≈ 0.95 m s⁻¹ in the direction from A toward B.

Key insight: average speed ≠ average velocity because the paths in the two legs are in opposite directions — displacement is less than total distance.

questions.question: Abdul drives to school at 20 km h⁻¹ and returns along the same route at 30 km h⁻¹. Calculate the average speed for the whole trip. Why is it NOT simply (20 + 30)/2 = 25 km h⁻¹?
questions.answer: Average speed = 24 km h⁻¹, not 25 km h⁻¹.
questions.solution:
Let one-way distance = d km.
Time going (at 20 km h⁻¹) = d/20 h.
Time returning (at 30 km h⁻¹) = d/30 h.
Total distance = 2d km.
Total time = d/20 + d/30 = 3d/60 + 2d/60 = 5d/60 = d/12 h.

Average speed = 2d / (d/12) = 2d × 12/d = 24 km h⁻¹.

Why not 25 km h⁻¹?
Simply averaging speeds (25 km h⁻¹) is incorrect because equal distances are covered at each speed, NOT equal times. The journey at 20 km h⁻¹ takes MORE time than at 30 km h⁻¹. So the slower speed contributes more to the total time. The correct method weights speeds by time, giving a harmonic mean: 2×20×30/(20+30) = 1200/50 = 24 km h⁻¹.

questions.question: An artificial satellite moves in a circular orbit of radius 42250 km and completes one revolution in 24 hours. Calculate its orbital speed.
questions.solution:
Given: r = 42250 km = 42,250,000 m; t = 24 h = 24 × 3600 = 86400 s.

Circumference = 2πr = 2 × 3.14159 × 42,250,000 ≈ 265,464,000 m ≈ 2.65 × 10⁸ m.

Speed v = 2πr / t = 2.65 × 10⁸ / 86400 ≈ 3067 m s⁻¹ ≈ 3.07 km s⁻¹.

This is a geostationary satellite — completing one orbit in exactly 24 h means it stays above the same point on Earth (since Earth also rotates once in 24 h). Even at 3.07 km/s, since the orbit is so large the angular speed matches Earth's rotation.

questions.question: A racing car at rest has uniform acceleration of 4 m s⁻². Another car B starts at the same time from the same point with a uniform velocity of 20 m s⁻¹. (i) After how many seconds does car A reach the same speed as car B? (ii) After how many seconds does car A cover the same distance as car B at that moment?
questions.answer: (i) Car A reaches 20 m s⁻¹ after t = 5 s. (ii) Car A covers the same distance as car B at t = 10 s.
questions.solution:
(i) Car A: v = u + at = 0 + 4t. Car B: constant velocity 20 m s⁻¹.
For equal speeds: 4t = 20 → t = 5 s. At t=5s, car A speed = car B speed = 20 m s⁻¹.

(ii) Distance by car A: s_A = ½ × 4 × t² = 2t².
    Distance by car B: s_B = 20t.
    Equal distances: 2t² = 20t → 2t = 20 → t = 10 s.
    At t=10s: s_A = 2 × 100 = 200 m; s_B = 20 × 10 = 200 m. ✓

Insight: car A is slower than B for the first 5 s (B has a head start on distance). Car A overtakes B after t = 10 s because it is consistently accelerating. Between t = 5s and t = 10s, car A is faster than B but B still has a distance advantage from the first 5 s.

questions.question: A velocity-time graph shows: from t=0 to t=4 s, velocity increases uniformly from 0 to 8 m s⁻¹. From t=4 s to t=8 s, velocity stays constant at 8 m s⁻¹. From t=8 s to t=12 s, velocity decreases uniformly from 8 m s⁻¹ to 0. Calculate: (i) acceleration in each phase, (ii) total distance covered.
questions.answer: (i) Phase 1: a = 2 m s⁻². Phase 2: a = 0. Phase 3: a = −2 m s⁻². (ii) Total distance = 64 m.
questions.solution:
(i) Acceleration = slope of v-t graph:
Phase 1 (0–4 s): a = (8 − 0)/(4 − 0) = 2 m s⁻². [Uniform acceleration]
Phase 2 (4–8 s): a = (8 − 8)/(8 − 4) = 0. [Uniform velocity]
Phase 3 (8–12 s): a = (0 − 8)/(12 − 8) = −8/4 = −2 m s⁻². [Uniform deceleration]

(ii) Distance = total area under v-t graph:
Phase 1 (triangle): ½ × 4 × 8 = 16 m.
Phase 2 (rectangle): 4 × 8 = 32 m.
Phase 3 (triangle): ½ × 4 × 8 = 16 m.
Total = 16 + 32 + 16 = 64 m.

Verification: average velocity over whole = total distance/total time = 64/12 ≈ 5.33 m s⁻¹.
